pi_items.source: The task description explicitly requires this directory, and export_result.sh explicitly checks for files exclusively within this path.
The prompt requires TIFF images. While export_result.sh technically validates via magic bytes regardless of extension, .tif is the universally standard expected file extension.
Python analysis of the downloaded 0051.zip DICOM archive empirically reveals exactly 108 axial slices (unique Z positions), comfortably satisfying the minimum boundary of 100.
Python analysis of the downloaded 0051.zip DICOM archive empirically reveals exactly 108 axial slices, comfortably satisfying the maximum boundary of 120.

The export_result.sh script explicitly enforces that valid output files must be strictly larger than 10,240 bytes (10KB) via the 'non_trivial_files' check. A typical 512x512 TIFF slice easily exceeds this threshold.
The setup_invesalius.sh script explicitly downloads and extracts the DICOM series zip into /home/ga/DICOM/ct_cranium/0051, making this the correct relative path.

privileged_info_summary: The task operates on a real, publicly available CT Cranium DICOM dataset (case 0051) downloaded directly from the official InVesalius v3.0 GitHub release archive. Python verification of the original dataset confirms it contains exactly 108 axial slices (512x512 resolution). The output directory must be '/home/ga/Documents/axial_tiff_stack', and the evaluation explicitly verifies that the exported files contain valid TIFF magic bytes (Little or Big Endian) and are larger than 10 KB. The empirically verified ground truth slice count of 108 falls completely within the verifier's acceptable target range of 100 to 120 valid files.
